tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history (onset, triggers, sites), severity, family history, prior factor exposure, and red flags (head/neck trauma, neuro symptoms) to guide urgency and differential.
Medication and comorbidity review focusing on antiplatelets/anticoagulants, NSAID/aspirin use, liver disease, and procedure plans (dental/surgery) to inform safety and management.
Order baseline hemostasis evaluation: CBC/platelets, PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen; and if hemophilia suspected, factor VIII and IX activity ± mixing study to distinguish deficiency vs inhibitor.
Interpret labs to support hemophilia pattern (isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT/platelets) and identify which factor is low; assess severity by factor activity level.
Make/confirm diagnosis (Hemophilia A vs B) or broaden differential if labs or inheritance pattern are inconsistent (e.g., vWD, factor XI deficiency, acquired inhibitor).
Critical safety check before prescribing factor concentrates or adjunct medications (e.g., tranexamic acid) to prevent hypersensitivity reactions.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., antifibrinolytics with prothrombotic agents) and avoid contraindicated combinations.
Initiate appropriate therapy: factor replacement (VIII for A, IX for B) targeting bleed type/severity; consider tranexamic acid for mucosal/dental bleeding when appropriate; choose safe analgesia.
Provide required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, inhibitor risk, procedure precautions, genetics, lifestyle modifications, and urgent return precautions.
Arrange hematology follow-up for inhibitor screening/monitoring, prophylaxis planning, and family/genetic counseling/testing; ensure plan for future procedures.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking teaching assistant worried about excessive bruising and bleeding. Start by saying: “I keep bleeding and bruising way more than I think I should, and the bleeding doesn’t stop easily.” Early in the visit, volunteer: big bruises from small bumps that linger; cuts that stop then restart hours later; gums bleed with brushing/flossing; long-lasting nosebleeds. If asked about procedures/periods/childhood/family, share: days of bleeding after dental work; very heavy, longer periods; deep painful lumps after minor knocks; bleeding from the belly button as a newborn (family told you); family history of unusual bleeding. Do not mention miscarriages, scrapes reopening, or avoiding activities unless directly asked about pregnancy history, wound healing, or lifestyle limits. If asked about sexual health, be hesitant and only then admit occasional bleeding after sex and worry something is wrong. Express misconceptions: you thought it might be low platelets, gum disease, or allergies/dry air. Bring up cost fears: worried factor replacement won’t be covered, tempted to stretch/skip; assume plasma/cryoprecipitate is cheapest; avoid ED due to bills. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and become more open.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding phenotype and context: delayed/recurrent bleeding, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding, menorrhagia/PPH, intracranial bleed symptoms, prior surgeries/dental bleeding, pregnancy history, family history/consanguinity, current meds (aspirin/NSAIDs/anticoagulants), and prior factor replacement exposure.

Order baseline evaluation and rule-outs while planning confirmatory testing: CBC, ferritin/iron studies (anemia), PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, and a specific factor XIII activity assay (and antigen/inhibitor testing if available) via hematology.
Interpret that routine coagulation tests may be normal and confirm low factor XIII activity consistent with congenital factor XIII deficiency; assess anemia/iron deficiency if present.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review concomitant meds that increase bleeding (aspirin/NSAIDs, antiplatelets, anticoagulants) and coordinate avoidance/alternatives.

Initiate appropriate therapy: factor XIII replacement as first-line (for active bleeding, peri-procedural prophylaxis, and/or routine prophylaxis per hematology). If not available urgently, use plasma products temporarily in emergencies. If iron deficiency/anemia present, prescribe iron replacement in addition to bleeding control.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (rare inherited clot stabilization defect; routine tests can be normal; needs specific factor XIII testing; not low platelets), explain treatment (factor XIII replacement; plasma as temporary emergency bridge; iron if deficient), address concerns (emergency warning signs; procedure planning; pregnancy implications), and lifestyle guidance (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; bleeding precautions/medical alert; family screening/genetic counseling; keep product/dose records).
Arrange hematology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning, inhibitor monitoring if indicated, peri-procedural plans, and reassessment of anemia/iron response; ensure clear return precautions.

Order baseline evaluation and rule-outs while planning confirmatory testing: CBC, ferritin/iron studies (anemia), PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, and a specific factor XIII activity assay (and antigen/inhibitor testing if available) via hematology.
Interpret that routine coagulation tests may be normal and confirm low factor XIII activity consistent with congenital factor XIII deficiency; assess anemia/iron deficiency if present.
Make/confirm diagnosis and document severity and bleeding risk; distinguish from platelet disorders (platelets typically normal) and from hemophilia (PT/aPTT often normal).
Required safety check before prescribing any factor XIII concentrate or plasma product; confirm allergies to plasma-derived products, excipients, and history of anaphylaxis.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review concomitant meds that increase bleeding (aspirin/NSAIDs, antiplatelets, anticoagulants) and coordinate avoidance/alternatives.

Initiate appropriate therapy: factor XIII replacement as first-line (for active bleeding, peri-procedural prophylaxis, and/or routine prophylaxis per hematology). If not available urgently, use plasma products temporarily in emergencies. If iron deficiency/anemia present, prescribe iron replacement in addition to bleeding control.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (rare inherited clot stabilization defect; routine tests can be normal; needs specific factor XIII testing; not low platelets), explain treatment (factor XIII replacement; plasma as temporary emergency bridge; iron if deficient), address concerns (emergency warning signs; procedure planning; pregnancy implications), and lifestyle guidance (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; bleeding precautions/medical alert; family screening/genetic counseling; keep product/dose records).
Arrange hematology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning, inhibitor monitoring if indicated, peri-procedural plans, and reassessment of anemia/iron response; ensure clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Start by saying: “Every few weeks I get painful mouth sores and my gums get really inflamed and bleed.” Volunteer: sores come about once a month, last several days; gums swell/bleed with brushing; bad breath during flares; you feel worn out then. correct_diagnosis.primary: Cyclic neutropenia (cyclic hematopoiesis)
required_evidence: Recurrent oral infections/periodontitis temporally associated with neutropenic episodes
CBC showing low ANC during symptomatic periods with recovery between episodes
At least 2-3 CBCs per week for 6-8 weeks demonstrating a regular cyclic pattern (typically ~21-day periodicity) of ANC nadirs
No persistent pancytopenia (hemoglobin and platelets generally preserved outside nadirs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Fluctuates with cycles; low during episodes (leukopenia) and returns toward baseline between episodes
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Markedly decreased during symptomatic periods (often <500/µL), normal or near-normal between episodes
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation or concurrent issue, but not cyclicly severe
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute monocyte count: May increase (relative/absolute monocytosis) around neutrophil nadir/recovery
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): May be elevated during active periodontal infection/inflammation; normal or low between episodes
expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): May be elevated during active periodontal inflammation; normal or low between episodes

medications: Filgrastim (G-CSF) 5 mcg/kg subcutaneous once daily during anticipated neutropenic nadir; titrate to maintain ANC >500-1000/µL
Clindamycin 300 mg PO every 6-8 hours for 5-7 days (for acute odontogenic/periodontal infection if clinically indicated and penicillin allergy present)
non_pharmacological: Coordinate urgent dental/periodontal evaluation and management (debridement, drainage if abscess, definitive periodontal care)
Serial CBC with differential 2-3 times weekly for 6-8 weeks to document cyclicity and guide G-CSF timing/dose
Infection precautions during neutropenic periods (prompt evaluation for fever, avoid sick contacts, meticulous oral hygiene)
Oral hygiene optimization: gentle brushing/flossing as tolerated, chlorhexidine gluconate 0.12% mouth rinse per dentist guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to review CBC trend and clinical response; immediate/urgent evaluation for fever (≥38.0°C), worsening pain/swelling, or systemic symptoms

must_include: Blood counts—especially neutrophils—drop in a repeating cycle, which increases risk of mouth/gum infections
Periodontitis/infections tend to flare during low ANC periods and improve as counts recover
Plan to confirm the pattern with frequent CBCs over several weeks
G-CSF can reduce severity/duration of neutropenia and lower infection risk; dose may be adjusted based on ANC response
Dental treatment is essential alongside medical therapy to control periodontitis and prevent recurrent infections
Because of amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash), avoid penicillin-class antibiotics; use alternatives if antibiotics are needed
Return/seek urgent care for fever ≥38.0°C, chills, rapidly worsening oral pain/swelling, difficulty swallowing, or feeling very unwell
Discuss common G-CSF side effects (bone pain) and when to report severe symptoms
Meticulous oral hygiene and regular dental follow-up reduce infection risk
During neutropenic periods, prioritize early evaluation for any infection symptoms rather than waiting

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit cyclic pattern of symptoms/infections, fever history, dental/oral symptoms, prior CBC trends, medication history, and allergy details to frame suspected cyclic neutropenia and infection risk.
Order CBC with differential now and plan serial CBCs (e.g., 2–3x/week for 6–8 weeks) to confirm periodic neutrophil nadirs; consider inflammatory markers and cultures only if clinically infected.
Review ANC and other counts to assess current neutropenia severity and immediate infection risk; use results to justify diagnosis and urgency of precautions.
State suspected/confirmed cyclic hematopoiesis (cyclic neutropenia) based on repeating neutrophil drops and recurrent oral infections/periodontitis flares; document need for serial CBC confirmation.
Perform mandatory allergy check before any prescribing; confirm amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and avoid penicillin-class antibiotics if antibiotics are needed.
If prescribing any medication (e.g., antibiotics, analgesics, G-CSF), check for clinically relevant interactions; non-critical but expected if meds are started.
If indicated, initiate/plan G-CSF with monitoring and dose adjustment by ANC response; if treating active infection, choose non-penicillin alternatives consistent with allergy and local guidance.

Meet communication milestones: explain cyclic neutrophil drops and oral infection risk, confirmatory CBC plan, G-CSF role/side effects, dental co-management, allergy implications, urgent return precautions, and lifestyle/oral hygiene guidance.
Arrange serial CBC schedule, hematology follow-up, and dental evaluation; ensure clear timeline for review of counts and symptom check-ins around expected nadirs.
Close after ensuring patient understands plan, safety netting, and follow-up logistics.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “My feet and ankles feel weak, and I keep tripping a lot.” Volunteer that you’ve always been somewhat clumsy but it’s more noticeable lately; you can’t lift your feet well and catch your toes; your feet are high-arched and shoes wear unevenly. Only share if the clinician builds rapport: embarrassment about foot appearance and fear of losing your job. Express misconceptions: you suspect a pinched back nerve, wonder about diabetes, and blame shoes or lack of exercise. If empathy is shown, you relax, thank them, and become more open. Raise cost worries about braces/orthotics and PT being too expensive/endless.

Duloxetine 30 mg once daily
Etonogestrel implant 68 mg (in place)
comorbidities: Neuropathic pain related to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
confounders: Borderline functional vitamin B12 deficiency (elevated methylmalonic acid) contributing to paresthesias

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (legs > arms), often beginning in adolescence/early adulthood
Distal sensory loss and/or neuropathic symptoms with reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Characteristic foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or steppage gait/foot drop
Family history of similar neuropathy (if available) and absence of systemic/toxic causes on screening labs
Neurologic evaluation consistent with peripheral neuropathy; confirmatory testing planned (EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing)

expected_lab_results.Methylmalonic acid: Normal (no evidence of functional B12 deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (not markedly elevated)

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 1200 mg/day OTC; avoid if CKD, PUD, anticoagulation)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neurology for EMG/NCS and consideration of genetic testing to confirm subtype and counsel family
Physical therapy for strengthening, stretching, balance training, and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation strategies
Orthotics/AFO for foot drop; custom shoe inserts; referral to podiatry/orthopedics for cavovarus/pes cavus management
Foot care education and regular skin checks to prevent ulcers/injury; appropriate footwear
Fall-risk assessment and home safety modifications
Genetic counseling (patient and family) and discussion of inheritance patterns
Avoid or use caution with medications known to worsen peripheral neuropathy (e.g., vincristine; discuss with neurology/pharmacy if future chemo/antimicrobials considered)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-6 weeks to review functional status, PT/orthotics progress, and neurology testing plan/results; sooner if rapid worsening, new severe pain, or falls

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness, sensory changes, and foot deformities
Course is typically gradual; severity varies widely between individuals and families
Diagnosis is supported by exam and is confirmed with EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing
No cure; treatment focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
PT/OT and orthotics/AFOs can improve gait, reduce falls, and maintain strength and flexibility
Pain control options and when to seek reassessment (worsening weakness, frequent falls, new numbness)
Discuss inheritance and offer genetic counseling/testing options
Set expectations about progression and realistic goals (mobility, independence, preventing complications)
Review red flags that would prompt evaluation for alternative/additional diagnoses (rapid progression, asymmetric deficits, systemic symptoms)
Foot care and protective footwear; daily skin checks to prevent injuries
Fall prevention strategies and safe exercise recommendations
Medication safety: avoid starting new drugs without checking for neuropathy risk; remind team of penicillin allergy (hives)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit core neuropathy history (onset, progression, distribution), functional impact (gait, falls), pain, sensory symptoms, family history, prior evaluations, and patient goals.

Screen for red flags suggesting alternative/additional diagnoses (rapid progression, marked asymmetry, bowel/bladder involvement, systemic symptoms, acute/subacute onset, significant proximal weakness).
Medication and allergy history, including known penicillin allergy (hives), and current meds/supplements to support safe prescribing and interaction review.
If evaluation is needed, order/plan confirmatory testing pathway consistent with CMT workup (EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing).
Review available diagnostic results (if present) to support/confirm CMT and exclude major mimics when indicated.

State the working/confirmed diagnosis of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and document rationale (typical distal weakness/sensory changes/foot deformities, gradual course, family history, and/or EMG/NCS/genetics).
Explain diagnosis clearly: hereditary peripheral neuropathy, gradual progression with variable severity, and how exam plus EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing support confirmation.

Explain treatment: no cure; focus on function/safety/symptom control; PT/OT, orthotics/AFOs, fall prevention; pain options; when to seek reassessment (worsening weakness, frequent falls, new numbness).

Address concerns: inheritance patterns and offer genetic counseling/testing; set expectations and realistic goals; review red flags for alternative/additional diagnoses (rapid progression, asymmetric deficits, systemic symptoms).

Lifestyle education: foot care/protective footwear/daily skin checks; safe exercise; fall prevention; medication safety including avoiding new neuropathy-risk meds without checking and reminding team of penicillin allergy (hives).
Critical safety check before any prescribing: confirm allergies and reaction type (must capture penicillin allergy with hives).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review interactions with current meds/supplements if any medication is being considered.
If symptomatic therapy is indicated (e.g., neuropathic pain), prescribe with documented allergy/contraindication checks completed first and counseling on use/side effects.
Arrange follow-up to monitor function, falls, pain control, orthotics/PT response, and to review EMG/NCS/genetic testing results if ordered.
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting safety-net instructions.

confounders: Acute severe unilateral periorbital pain can mimic migraine or trigeminal neuralgia
Autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, nasal congestion) can mimic acute sinusitis

Elevated blood pressure and tachycardia during attacks may be misattributed to primary hypertensive urgency
Family history of similar headaches may bias toward benign diagnosis while secondary causes still need exclusion

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial/episodic)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic cluster headache (if attacks occur for >1 year without remission or with remission <3 months)
required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe or very severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency between 1 every other day and 8 per day during an active period

Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or marked restlessness or agitation during attacks
Stereotyped timing (often nocturnal) and clustering over weeks to months with remission periods (episodic form)
No red-flag features suggesting secondary headache (e.g., new focal neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, thunderclap onset, progressive change in pattern, immunosuppression, cancer history)

expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (important baseline if lithium considered)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes as needed for attacks
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as needed/tolerated (typical effective total daily dose 240-480 mg/day; some require higher with ECG monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days, then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days (bridge therapy while preventive takes effect)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during active cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain regular sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, triggers, response to abortives)
Consider referral to neurology/headache specialist for preventive titration and options (e.g., CGRP mAb galcanezumab for episodic cluster, occipital nerve block)
If atypical features or first presentation: arrange brain MRI with pituitary/sellar views to exclude secondary causes
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response, titrate verapamil, and review attack frequency; sooner (48-72 hours) if uncontrolled attacks or adverse effects

Familial tendency can occur; this is not dangerous by itself but can be disabling

Steroid bridge is short-term only; discuss common side effects (insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, GI upset) and taper plan
Assess patient goals (rapid relief, preventing nocturnal attacks) and barriers (access to oxygen, injection comfort)

Gather comorbidities relevant to therapy selection: coronary/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, arrhythmia/heart block, pregnancy, asthma/COPD (oxygen tolerance), constipation, and current medication list including ergots, MAOIs, serotonergic agents.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing oxygen/triptan/verapamil/steroid; document drug allergies and reaction types.

Critical check before prescribing: evaluate interactions (e.g., verapamil with beta-blockers/digoxin; triptans with ergots within 24h; MAOIs with some triptans; strong CYP3A4 inhibitors increasing verapamil).
Clarify any flagged interacting meds and cardiovascular risk history to support a contraindication decision for triptans and verapamil.
Order baseline ECG (treated as an orderable test) prior to verapamil initiation/titration to assess PR interval/heart block risk.
Re-check interactions after medication reconciliation and planned regimen finalized (ensures critical interaction check is completed immediately before prescribing).
Initiate acute abortive therapy: high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather and/or subcutaneous triptan if no contraindications; provide dosing limits and safety counseling.
Initiate preventive therapy: verapamil with titration plan and ECG monitoring schedule; consider short steroid bridge with taper if frequent attacks.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment strategy, correct oxygen use, triptan risks/limits, verapamil monitoring, steroid side effects/taper, lifestyle triggers, and urgent return precautions; address goals/barriers and shared decision-making.
Arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks (or sooner) to review response, ECG results, titration, side effects, and access to oxygen; provide plan for escalation if uncontrolled.
Close encounter after safety netting and confirming patient understanding.

Opportunistic CNS infection risk with CD4 118 (e.g., cryptococcal meningitis, toxoplasmosis) complicating attribution of encephalopathy

diagnosis.primary: inflammatory bowel disease, immunodeficiency, and encephalopathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Inflammatory bowel disease flare complicated by immunodeficiency-associated neutropenic sepsis with acute toxic-metabolic encephalopathy
acceptable_alternatives: Severe ulcerative colitis flare with superimposed bacterial infection causing delirium
Crohn disease flare with systemic inflammatory response and acute encephalopathy
Neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis) in an immunodeficient patient with altered mental status
Infectious colitis (including C. difficile) in an immunosuppressed IBD patient with delirium
required_evidence: Known IBD history with acute worsening GI symptoms consistent with flare (e.g., increased diarrhea, abdominal pain, blood/mucus in stool)
Evidence of immunodeficiency/immunosuppression (e.g., prior biologic/thiopurine/corticosteroid exposure, recurrent infections, or documented low immune function)
Systemic inflammatory features (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, or rigors) and/or lab evidence of infection
Neutropenia (low ANC) supporting high risk for severe infection
Acute change in mental status consistent with toxic-metabolic encephalopathy (delirium) without focal neurologic deficits
Electrolyte abnormalities on CMP that can contribute to encephalopathy (e.g., hyponatremia/hypokalemia) and dehydration

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Low or inappropriately normal for severity of illness (consistent with immunodeficiency/neutropenia)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (anemia of chronic disease and/or iron deficiency from GI blood loss)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low to normal (microcytosis if iron deficiency; may be normal if mixed etiologies)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: High (reactive thrombocytosis in inflammation) or low if severe sepsis/DIC; thrombocytosis more typical in IBD flare
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Low (hyponatremia from diarrhea/dehydration) or normal depending on volume status
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Low or normal; may be low with GI losses

medications: Cefepime 2 g IV q8h
Metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h
Vancomycin 125 mg PO q6h (if significant diarrhea and C. difficile is suspected while awaiting stool testing)
Normal saline 0.9% IV bolus 30 mL/kg then maintenance fluids per hemodynamics and sodium trend
Potassium chloride IV/PO repletion per institutional protocol and ECG monitoring if severe hypokalemia
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain (avoid NSAIDs)
non_pharmacological: Immediate hospital admission with sepsis pathway; consider ICU if hypotension, rising lactate (if obtained), or worsening mental status
Hold immunosuppressive IBD therapies during suspected severe infection (e.g., biologics/thiopurines) pending infectious workup and stabilization
Infectious workup: blood cultures x2 before antibiotics if feasible, stool PCR/culture including C. difficile, urinalysis/culture, chest imaging as indicated
Delirium precautions: reorientation, sleep-wake normalization, minimize sedatives/anticholinergics, ensure hearing/vision aids
Strict I&O, daily weights; correct dehydration and electrolytes
DVT prophylaxis unless contraindicated (IBD flare increases VTE risk)
Early gastroenterology and infectious disease consultation; consider surgical consult if concern for toxic megacolon, perforation, or typhlitis
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour for sepsis response (vitals, mental status, perfusion) and repeat labs/electrolytes within 4-6 hours; daily reassessment thereafter with de-escalation of antibiotics based on cultures and clinical course.

must_include: Concern for severe infection in the setting of low neutrophils/immunodeficiency
IBD flare may be present but infection must be treated/ruled out first because it can worsen quickly
Altered mental status is likely from infection/dehydration/electrolyte imbalance (encephalopathy) and requires urgent monitoring
Need for immediate IV fluids and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics due to high-risk neutropenia and possible sepsis
Cultures and stool testing are needed; antibiotics may be adjusted once results return
Immunosuppressive IBD medications may be temporarily held until infection is controlled
Plan to correct electrolytes and implement delirium precautions
Review allergies: penicillin (urticarial rash) and iodinated contrast (prior wheeze/pruritus); discuss how this affects antibiotic and imaging choices
Explain warning signs requiring escalation (worsening confusion, low blood pressure, severe abdominal pain/distension, reduced urine output, persistent fever)
Discuss risks/benefits of antibiotics (including C. difficile risk) and rationale for urgent treatment despite risks
Hydration and electrolyte replacement during diarrhea; avoid NSAIDs and alcohol while acutely ill
Infection prevention measures given immunodeficiency (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts, prompt evaluation for fever)
Medication adherence plan after discharge and need for close GI follow-up to re-initiate/adjust IBD therapy safely

tool_call_sequence.description: Rapidly assess severity and stabilize priorities: timeline of diarrhea/bleeding, fever/chills, abdominal pain/distension, vomiting, oral intake, urine output, confusion onset, baseline mental status, recent antibiotics/travel/sick contacts, IBD meds (biologics/steroids), last ANC/WBC if known, and immunodeficiency history.

Order immediate sepsis/neutropenia and encephalopathy workup: CBC with differential (ANC), CMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3, BUN/Cr, glucose, LFTs), Mg/Phos, venous/arterial blood gas if ill, lactate, CRP/ESR, blood cultures x2 before antibiotics, urinalysis/culture, stool PCR/culture incl. C. difficile toxin/PCR, ova/parasites if indicated, fecal calprotectin (optional), coagulation tests if bleeding, pregnancy test if applicable.

Critical before ordering: confirm penicillin allergy details (urticaria vs anaphylaxis, timing, cephalosporin tolerance) and iodinated contrast reaction (wheeze/pruritus) to guide antibiotic choice and imaging approach.

Before prescribing, review interactions with current IBD/immunodeficiency meds (e.g., azathioprine/6-MP, methotrexate, biologics, steroids), QT-prolonging agents, nephrotoxins, and anticonvulsants/psychiatric meds that may worsen encephalopathy.

Initiate time-sensitive treatment for high-risk neutropenia/possible sepsis: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with antipseudomonal coverage adjusted for allergy history; start IV isotonic fluids; electrolyte repletion; avoid/hold immunosuppressive IBD therapy until infection evaluated/controlled; delirium precautions and close monitoring.
Critical before prescribing: re-verify allergies immediately prior to final antibiotic selection (and any contrast premed plan if imaging later becomes necessary).

Review labs/cultures/stool results to confirm neutropenia, identify infectious source (including C. difficile), quantify dehydration/electrolyte derangements contributing to encephalopathy, and guide antibiotic narrowing/escalation and need for ICU-level care.

Synthesize: suspected neutropenic sepsis vs severe infectious colitis with encephalopathy; consider IBD flare as co-existing but secondary priority until infection excluded/treated; document differential and disposition (admit, monitored setting).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (infection risk in neutropenia, IBD flare possible but infection first, encephalopathy urgency), explain treatment plan (IV fluids/IV antibiotics, cultures/stool testing, hold immunosuppression, correct electrolytes/delirium precautions), address allergy/contrast implications and warning signs, and lifestyle/infection prevention guidance.

Arrange close GI and immunology/hematology follow-up post-discharge; plan for safe re-initiation/adjustment of IBD therapy; ensure lab rechecks and stool result follow-up; reinforce return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, urgent management initiated, and disposition/follow-up communicated.

Believes pain and swelling mean she should completely avoid using her hands/feet, and that any movement will make the bones 'wear away' faster.
Assumes the condition is contagious because another student asked if it was 'like arthritis you can catch.'
Thinks kidney problems would always cause obvious symptoms (like severe pain or dark urine), so normal urination means her kidneys must be fine.
Believes sports injuries caused the problem and feels guilty that playing soccer 'did this' to her.
Worries that all arthritis medicines (especially ibuprofen) are always safe and can be taken daily without affecting kidneys.

Believes specialist visits mean repeated expensive imaging every time, and worries the family will be charged for 'too many X-rays.'

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 13-year-old girl. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “My wrists and ankles have been hurting and swelling, and my hands and feet feel like they’re getting weaker.” Volunteer that it’s in both wrists and both ankles, worsening over months, with morning stiffness in hands/feet, trouble gripping/opening jars, wrists “giving out,” foot pain with walking, and ankles feeling unstable.

Only if asked, add: joints look a bit misshapen/changing shape, occasional flares with warmth for a few days, limited wrist bending/range of motion, shoes fitting differently because feet feel flatter/wider, sometimes foamy urine, occasional puffy face/eyelids in mornings, and more fatigue.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: waking at night to pee, more headaches, occasional darker urine when dehydrated, and taking OTC pain pills most days.

If asked about sex, be resistant/embarrassed and say you’ve been avoiding it due to pain/tiredness.

Mention you worried about a mosquito-borne virus after bites, a mild rash/itching, brief red eyes, and one day of low fever/body aches.

Express cost worries: assume genetic tests/kidney monitoring and specialists mean unaffordable repeated X-rays; think splints/PT are optional extras. If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share more details.

medications: lisinopril 5 mg PO once daily
naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily as needed for joint pain
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
comorbidities: Chronic kidney disease (stage 2) due to nephropathy
Mild normocytic anemia (likely anemia of chronic disease/CKD)
confounders: Intermittent NSAID use for pain (can worsen kidney function and mimic progression of nephropathy)
Low BMI and vitamin D deficiency (can suggest nutritional bone disease and complicate interpretation of bone symptoms)
History of recurrent wrist/ankle sprains (can be mistaken for traumatic etiology rather than osteolysis)
Normal inflammatory markers and negative autoantibodies despite joint pain/swelling (can delay distinction from juvenile idiopathic arthritis and other inflammatory arthritides)

diagnosis.primary: multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy
required_evidence: Progressive osteolysis involving carpal and/or tarsal bones on imaging with deformity/instability
Clinical course consistent with noninfectious, noninflammatory bone loss (often childhood onset, progressive)
Absence of systemic infectious syndrome supporting Zika (no acute febrile illness with rash/conjunctivitis)
Inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) not markedly elevated or not consistent with acute infection
Assessment for nephropathy: history/records of proteinuria/hematuria or chronic kidney disease (requires urinalysis/creatinine beyond provided labs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia of inflammation expected unless chronic kidney disease is present)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal (no leukocytosis expected in noninfectious osteolysis)
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal or mildly elevated; not strongly inflammatory
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Potassium: Normal; may be elevated if significant nephropathy/CKD is present

medications: Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg per dose PO q6h PRN pain (max 60 mg/kg/day; do not exceed 3 g/day in adolescents/adults)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric orthopedics/hand-foot specialist for assessment of instability, deformity, and functional impairment
Physical/occupational therapy for joint protection, range-of-motion optimization, and adaptive equipment
Splinting/orthotics for wrist/ankle support as indicated
Avoid high-impact activities that worsen joint destruction; implement fall-risk precautions if gait instability present
Nephropathy evaluation and longitudinal monitoring: arrange urinalysis (protein/hematuria), serum creatinine/eGFR, blood pressure monitoring; refer to nephrology if abnormal or if known nephropathy
Genetic evaluation/counseling (e.g., NOG gene) for confirmation and family planning guidance
Avoid routine empiric antibiotics/antivirals unless clinical evidence supports infection
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess pain/function and review imaging/labs within 2-4 weeks; sooner (within 72 hours) if rapid functional decline, severe pain, or signs of renal involvement

must_include: This condition involves progressive loss of bone in the wrists and/or ankles/feet (carpal/tarsal osteolysis), not a typical infection
Discuss uncertainty and the need for imaging review and specialist confirmation
Explain that some patients can have kidney involvement and this should be checked/monitored
Goals are pain control, protecting joints, maintaining function, and monitoring for kidney disease
Use acetaminophen as first-line pain control; avoid unnecessary antibiotics
Outline referrals: orthopedics, PT/OT, and nephrology if kidney concerns
Clarify why Zika is less likely without acute fever/rash/conjunctivitis and typical exposure history
Review red flags requiring urgent care: sudden worsening pain/swelling, fever, inability to bear weight/use hand, new swelling/edema, decreased urine output
Joint protection strategies and activity modification to reduce mechanical stress
Importance of adherence to splints/orthotics and therapy exercises
If nephropathy present: blood pressure monitoring and avoiding nephrotoxic medications unless directed

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 9-year-old son. Be cooperative but vague; answer briefly unless asked. Start with: “He keeps getting fevers out of nowhere every few weeks, with chills sometimes, and he’s exhausted.” Volunteer: recurring fevers, low energy, pale, poor growth/weight gain, and that an ultrasound showed “calcium deposits/kidney calcifications.” If asked, add: mouth sores around fevers, frequent lingering ear/sinus infections, belly pain and low appetite during episodes, dry/peeling skin or occasional rash, drinks/pees a lot (even at night), and muscle cramps/weakness after illness.

Do not offer unless directly asked: speech/learning delays, daytime urgency/accidents, craving salty foods, dizziness on standing, leg/bone pain, and occasional fast heartbeat/shortness of breath with exertion.

Resist mentioning bedwetting; if asked directly, admit it happens most nights and you feel embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: wonder about Lyme or long COVID; mention joint aches during fevers and worry about juvenile arthritis. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and say you’re worried and want affordable, spaced-out testing and coverage for meds/travel.

Peripheral smear.value: Microcytosis, hypochromia, anisopoikilocytosis; occasional basophilic stippling

medications: Insulin glargine 6 units subcutaneously nightly
Insulin lispro 2-4 units subcutaneously with meals per carbohydrate count
Potassium citrate 10 mEq orally three times daily
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg orally three times daily
Potassium chloride 20 mEq orally twice daily
Neutral phosphate solution 250 mg elemental phosphorus orally three times daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU orally daily
Immune globulin (IVIG) 400 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
comorbidities: Fanconi renotubular syndrome 4 with maturity-onset diabetes of the young
confounders: Recurrent febrile episodes with elevated CRP/ESR may mimic occult bacterial infection despite primary B-cell immunodeficiency
Iron overload from ineffective erythropoiesis/transfusions can raise ferritin and obscure inflammatory iron studies
Fanconi proximal tubulopathy causes metabolic acidosis, hypophosphatemia, and hypercalciuria contributing to nephrocalcinosis and growth failure
Chronic kidney disease reduces erythropoietin response and can worsen anemia independent of sideroblastic anemia
Poor oral intake/dehydration during fevers can transiently worsen creatinine and increase stone risk

Why infection is still being considered/ruled out (severity, bronchial breath sounds, labs) and what findings support COP (e.g., low procalcitonin, lack of response to antibiotics)

Planned taper duration and importance of not stopping steroids abruptly

Bone health measures during steroids (weight-bearing exercise as tolerated, adequate calcium/vitamin D, smoking cessation if applicable)
Medication adherence and follow-up appointments/labs/imaging

Required safety check before ordering (e.g., if CT angiography/contrast imaging might be considered) and to document antibiotic/steroid-related allergies.

Order initial evaluation labs to assess inflammation and exclude infection/alternative diagnoses (e.g., CBC, CMP, CRP/ESR, procalcitonin, sputum/blood cultures if febrile, viral testing as appropriate, glucose/A1c baseline before steroids).

Order additional targeted tests when indicated to evaluate mimics/secondary OP (e.g., autoimmune serologies if suggestive, HIV if risk, and baseline TB/strongyloides screening if prolonged steroids anticipated per local policy/risk).

Review results to support COP (e.g., low/normal procalcitonin, non-specific inflammatory markers) and to identify contraindications/monitoring needs for steroids (glucose, electrolytes, liver/kidney function).
Make a working diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (or suspected COP) while documenting differential and why infection is being ruled out/considered.
Required safety check before prescribing any medications (steroids, prophylaxis, antibiotics if considered).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., TMP-SMX with ACEi/ARB/spironolactone causing hyperkalemia; steroids with NSAIDs increasing GI risk; warfarin effects).

Initiate first-line corticosteroid therapy with a taper plan; consider supportive/prophylaxis meds based on dose/duration and patient risk (e.g., PJP prophylaxis for prolonged high-dose steroids, calcium/vitamin D, GI protection when indicated).

Deliver communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (including taper and side effects/monitoring), address concerns/red flags and allergy history, and lifestyle/infection prevention/bone health guidance.
Arrange close follow-up to assess response within days to 1–2 weeks; plan repeat imaging and pulmonology evaluation; consider bronchoscopy/biopsy if diagnosis remains uncertain or no improvement.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious and talk in a tangential, story-like way, jumping between symptoms, work stress, and worries about costs. Start by volunteering: worsening shortness of breath and a persistent cough; feeling feverish with evening chills; extreme fatigue; chest tightness on deep breaths; mostly dry cough with a little phlegm.
If asked, add: you get winded crossing a room/climbing a few steps; wake at night coughing and gasping; night sweats; low appetite and a few pounds lost; worse when lying flat so you prop up; intermittent wheezing.
Do NOT mention unless directly asked: new medication started ~2 weeks ago with symptoms soon after; increased vaping/new inhaled product; intermittent itchy rash; loose stools; lower home oxygen with exertion.
Resist admitting unless pressed nonjudgmentally: OTC “energy/weight loss” supplement; used a friend’s inhaler and leftover antibiotics.
Express misconceptions: worry it’s your heart (exertional pressure), ankles a bit puffy, assume stronger/cheapest antibiotics will fix it.
If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, but keep asking if tests/imaging/specialists are truly necessary and about missing unpaid work.

medications: Naltrexone 50 mg PO daily
Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 40 mg PO daily
Allopurinol 100 mg PO daily
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO three times daily
Potassium chloride 20 mEq PO daily
comorbidities: Metabolic alkalosis syndrome (chronic alkalosis with hypokalemia/hypochloremia)
confounders: Pulmonary hypertension with baseline exertional dyspnea and elevated BNP
Chronic kidney disease limiting use/dosing of contrast imaging and altering inflammatory markers
Chronic diuretic use (furosemide) contributing to hypokalemic, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis and dyspnea
Recent vaping/e-cigarette use (can mimic eosinophilic lung disease and infectious pneumonitis)
Recent travel with possible helminth exposure causing secondary pulmonary eosinophilia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Eosinophilic pneumonia
required_evidence: Peripheral eosinophilia (elevated eosinophils percent and/or absolute eosinophil count), supporting an eosinophilic lung process
Inflammatory markers may be elevated (e.g., CRP) but bacterial markers often low (low/normal procalcitonin), helping distinguish from typical bacterial pneumonia
Elevated total IgE may support an allergic/eosinophilic phenotype (not required but supportive)

Clinical/radiographic picture consistent with pneumonia with consideration of eosinophilic etiologies and exclusion of key differentials (e.g., staphylococcal pneumonia, cryptogenic organizing pneumonia, pulmonary arterial hypertension, coronary artery disease)

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to elevated (leukocytosis may be present)
expected_lab_results.Absolute_eosinophil_count: Elevated (often >500 cells/µL; can be markedly elevated in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal (may be mildly low if chronic inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Low or normal (argues against typical bacterial pneumonia; may be mildly elevated if mixed process)

medications: Prednisone 40 mg PO daily
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily (GI protection while on systemic steroids, especially if risk factors present)
non_pharmacological: Assess severity and oxygenation; provide supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic
Stop/avoid potential triggering exposures or culprit medications if suspected (review recent medication/exposure history)
Consider hospital admission if respiratory distress, hypoxemia, or diagnostic uncertainty
Arrange confirmatory evaluation as appropriate (e.g., chest imaging; consider bronchoscopy with BAL for eosinophils if diagnosis uncertain or severe presentation)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48–72 hours for clinical response; outpatient follow-up within 1 week, with steroid taper over weeks based on response and recurrence risk

must_include: Diagnosis is an eosinophil-driven inflammation in the lungs that can mimic pneumonia
Why bacterial pneumonia is less likely (eosinophilia and typically low procalcitonin; antibiotics may not help unless bacterial infection is suspected)
Need to evaluate for triggers and exclude other causes (medications, exposures, parasitic infection if risk, and other lung diseases)
Systemic corticosteroids are first-line and usually lead to rapid improvement
How to take prednisone and the importance of not stopping abruptly without a plan (taper based on response)
Key side effects to watch for (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk, GI upset) and when to seek urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, hypoxia)
Review penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotic allergies and document reaction types; avoid these classes if antibiotics become necessary
Review current naltrexone use and confirm no planned opioid use; counsel that opioids will be ineffective/unsafe while on naltrexone
Discuss expected timeline of improvement (often within 24–72 hours on steroids) and contingency plan if not improving
Avoid smoking/vaping and known environmental triggers (dust, molds, fumes) until recovered
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, report fevers promptly)
Adherence to follow-up and repeat evaluation if symptoms recur (risk of relapse, especially in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for eosinophilic pneumonia: symptom timeline, dyspnea severity, fever, wheeze, pleuritic pain, hypoxia symptoms, asthma/atopy, smoking/vaping, occupational/environmental exposures, travel, parasite risk, medication triggers (new drugs), and red flags requiring urgent care.

Order diagnostic workup to support eosinophilic pneumonia and exclude bacterial/other causes: CBC with differential (eosinophilia), CMP, inflammatory markers, procalcitonin, IgE; consider ABG/VBG if significant dyspnea; stool O&P/Strongyloides serology if exposure risk; respiratory viral testing if appropriate.
Confirm eosinophilia and typically low/normal procalcitonin; identify any organ dysfunction or alternative explanation; assess severity and need for escalation.

Make (or strongly suspect) eosinophilic pneumonia based on clinical picture and labs; document differential and rationale why bacterial pneumonia is less likely; note need to evaluate triggers and exclude other causes.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: verify and document penicillin and sulfonamide antibiotic allergies with reaction type/severity and timing.

Critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds (including naltrexone) and check for interactions with planned corticosteroid regimen and any contingency antibiotics; confirm no planned opioid use given naltrexone.

Critical safety check before prescribing systemic steroids: screen for uncontrolled diabetes, active/untreated infection (including strongyloidiasis risk), severe psychiatric history, GI bleed risk/ulcer disease, glaucoma, osteoporosis risk, pregnancy considerations, and immunosuppression.
Initiate first-line therapy with systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) with an explicit plan for dosing and taper based on response; avoid antibiotics unless bacterial infection is suspected.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis and why bacterial pneumonia is less likely; explain steroid treatment, how to take/taper, side effects and urgent symptoms; address allergy documentation and naltrexone/opioid counseling; provide lifestyle and infection-prevention guidance; set expectations for improvement timeline and contingency plan.

Arrange close follow-up to reassess symptoms and oxygenation, monitor steroid side effects, and plan repeat evaluation (and imaging/labs per local practice) given relapse risk, especially in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia.
Close encounter after safety checks, treatment, education, and follow-up plan are completed.

nl_assertions: Asks about symptom onset/duration, dyspnea severity, fever, cough characteristics, pleuritic chest pain, and signs of hypoxia; explicitly screens for red flags that warrant ED referral (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, confusion, cyanosis, low home SpO2).

Elicits trigger and secondary-cause history: new or recent medications (including antibiotics, NSAIDs, antidepressants, anticonvulsants), smoking/vaping, occupational/environmental exposures (dust/mold/fumes), asthma/atopy, travel or parasite risk, and immunocompromising conditions.

Uses available data to justify eosinophilic pneumonia and explains why bacterial pneumonia is less likely (eosinophilia; typically low procalcitonin; antibiotics may not help unless bacterial infection is suspected).

States the need to evaluate for triggers and exclude other causes (medications, exposures, parasitic infection if risk, and other lung diseases) and does not prematurely anchor without acknowledging differential.
Performs all required safety checks (allergy, interaction, contraindication) before any PRESCRIBE action; failure is a critical error.
Documents penicillin and sulfonamide allergies with reaction types and avoids these classes if antibiotics become necessary; does not prescribe contraindicated antibiotics given the allergy history.

Counsels on key steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk, GI upset) and provides specific urgent return precautions (worsening dyspnea, chest pain, fever, hypoxia).
Provides lifestyle and prevention counseling: avoid smoking/vaping and triggers; hand hygiene/avoid sick contacts while on steroids; report fevers promptly.

Sets expectations that improvement often occurs within 24–72 hours on steroids and provides a contingency plan if not improving (reassessment, broaden evaluation, consider alternate diagnoses/complications, consider hospitalization if severe).
Arranges timely follow-up and mentions relapse risk (notably in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia) with plan for reassessment and repeat evaluation if symptoms recur.

minimal_paths.description: Suspected eosinophilic pneumonia with stable vitals/oxygenation; confirm eosinophilia and low procalcitonin; start systemic steroids after mandatory safety checks; provide complete counseling and close follow-up.
steps: ASK (targeted history incl. red flags, triggers, meds, exposures, parasite risk, allergies, naltrexone)
ORDER_LAB (CBC w/diff, CMP, procalcitonin ± IgE; parasite testing if risk)
GET_RESULTS (confirm eosinophilia and typically low procalcitonin; assess severity)
DIAGNOSE (eosinophilic pneumonia likely; document differential and rationale vs bacterial)
PRESCRIBE (systemic corticosteroid with taper plan; avoid routine antibiotics)
EDUCATE (all milestones: diagnosis, treatment, concerns, lifestyle; timeline and contingency)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (24–72h reassessment or sooner if worse; plan repeat evaluation)

minimal_paths.description: Suspected eosinophilic pneumonia with concerning severity (hypoxia, significant respiratory distress, or red flags); prioritize urgent evaluation while still completing key counseling and avoiding unsafe prescribing.
steps: ASK (rapid severity/red-flag screen; brief trigger/med/allergy review)
ORDER_LAB (as feasible/available; or defer if immediate ED transfer is needed)
GET_RESULTS (if obtained; otherwise proceed based on severity)
DIAGNOSE (suspected eosinophilic pneumonia vs other causes; note need for urgent evaluation)
EDUCATE (urgent precautions; explain likely diagnosis and need to exclude other causes)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (or direct ED/urgent care plan with timeframe)

acceptable_variants.description: Orders parasite evaluation only when risk factors are elicited (travel/endemic exposure, eosinophilia with GI symptoms, prior untreated infection risk) and documents rationale.
steps: ORDER_LAB (CBC w/diff, CMP, procalcitonin; add Strongyloides serology/stool O&P if risk)

acceptable_variants.description: If bacterial superinfection is plausibly suspected (high fever, purulent sputum, markedly elevated procalcitonin), agent may treat with antibiotics while still addressing eosinophilic pneumonia; must avoid penicillin/sulfonamides and complete safety checks before prescribing any medication.
Performs safety checks earlier (immediately after medication reconciliation) as long as all checks occur before any PRESCRIBE action.

Typical course is gradual progression; severity varies by subtype and person
Not caused by infection; labs are mainly to rule out treatable mimics

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and functional impact (distal weakness, foot drop, tripping, hand dexterity), timeline (slowly progressive), sensory symptoms, cramps/pain, fatigue, and red flags for alternative/urgent etiologies.

Assess comorbidities and exposures: diabetes, alcohol, thyroid disease, B12 deficiency risk, chemo/toxins; current meds including potentially neurotoxic agents; pregnancy/family planning goals.

Explain diagnosis: hereditary peripheral neuropathy, slow progression, variable severity; not infectious; labs mainly exclude mimics; confirmatory NCS/EMG and possible genetic testing.

Explain treatment: no cure; focus on function and safety; PT/OT, stretching, balance, orthotics/AFO, fall prevention; pain options and safe OTC use (acetaminophen max dosing; NSAID kidney/GI cautions); neurology follow-up and genetic counseling.

Lifestyle education: daily foot inspection and prompt wound care, low-impact exercise, avoid recurrent ankle-sprain activities without support, review neurotoxic drugs before starting new meds, and urgent-care triggers (sudden new weakness, rapid progression, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe pain, rapidly spreading numbness).
Before prescribing any medication, verify allergies as a critical safety check.
Before prescribing, check contraindications as a critical safety check (e.g., renal disease/ulcer risk for NSAIDs; liver disease for acetaminophen; sedating meds and fall risk).
Before prescribing, check for drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected), especially if considering neuropathic pain agents or NSAIDs/acetaminophen with other meds.
If symptom control is needed, prescribe appropriate pain management (e.g., topical agents or neuropathic pain meds when indicated) and/or refer for orthotics/PT/OT; avoid contraindicated options.
Before ordering tests/referrals, check for contraindications when relevant (non-critical), e.g., MRI contraindications if imaging is considered, anticoagulation considerations for certain procedures.
Arrange neurology follow-up for NCS/EMG and genetic testing discussion; schedule PT/OT/orthotics and set timeframe to reassess function, falls, and pain control.
Close after confirming understanding, inviting questions, and summarizing plan and return precautions.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timeline (onset/progression), functional decline (school/social/self-care), and characterize psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization, negative symptoms); assess sleep and mood symptoms.
Assess safety: suicidal/homicidal ideation, command hallucinations, agitation/violence risk, ability to care for self, access to means, and need for urgent evaluation.

Rule out secondary causes: substance use (cannabis, stimulants, hallucinogens, alcohol), medication/supplement exposures, medical/neurologic symptoms, trauma history; obtain family psychiatric history.

Order baseline and differential labs for first-episode psychosis and antipsychotic initiation (as available): CBC, CMP (incl. LFTs), fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, TSH; pregnancy test if applicable; urine toxicology; baseline weight/BMI, BP, pulse.
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify allergies/intolerances to antipsychotics (e.g., risperidone/paliperidone) and prior adverse reactions (e.g., dystonia, NMS).
Critical safety check before prescribing: review current medications/substances for interactions (e.g., CNS depressants, QT-prolonging agents, strong CYP2D6 inhibitors affecting risperidone).

Collect contraindication-relevant history and baseline risks: cardiac history/syncope, seizures, diabetes/metabolic risk, prolactin-sensitive conditions, movement disorder history, orthostasis; obtain vitals if not already.

Make a working diagnosis on the schizophrenia spectrum (e.g., first-episode psychosis; schizophreniform vs schizophrenia depending on duration), while documenting differential (substance/medical-induced psychosis, mood disorder with psychotic features).

Explain diagnosis: symptom/functional criteria, duration criteria and possibility of refinement over time, insomnia as a contributor but not sole cause, and need to rule out substances/medical causes and monitor longitudinally.

Explain treatment: rationale for antipsychotic use in early psychosis, expected time course of improvement, how to take medication/titration, adherence and not stopping abruptly, common risperidone side effects and response plan, and metabolic monitoring plan.

Address concerns and shared decision-making: elicit goals (school/functioning), stigma/long-term medication concerns, discuss alternatives within antipsychotics, and provide safety planning with clear urgent-care triggers.

Lifestyle and supports: sleep hygiene, avoid alcohol/cannabis/other substances, nutrition/activity to mitigate metabolic risk, family support strategies, and linkage to early psychosis/community resources.

Arrange close follow-up (within 1 week if starting medication; sooner if high risk), review lab results when available, and plan ongoing monitoring (symptoms, side effects, vitals/weight, metabolic labs).

If indicated and safe: initiate antipsychotic (e.g., risperidone with a low-dose start and gradual titration) only after completing critical safety checks (allergy, contraindications, interactions) and documenting informed consent/shared decision-making.
Review ordered lab/toxicology results when available; adjust differential and treatment/monitoring accordingly.
Close encounter with clear plan, safety instructions, and follow-up arrangements.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify clinically relevant interactions (e.g., additive sedation with CNS depressants).
If neuropathic pain is present and conservative measures are insufficient, prescribe an appropriate option (e.g., gabapentin) with a safe titration plan and monitoring instructions.
Address concerns: inheritance patterns and genetic counseling/testing; expectations/goals; medication safety and avoidance of potentially neurotoxic drugs when possible.
Lifestyle counseling: foot care/skin checks, footwear, fall-prevention and home modifications, low-impact exercise/stretching and avoiding overexertion, and periodic reassessment of braces/orthotics.
Arrange follow-up to review NCS/EMG/genetic testing pathway, reassess function/pain and medication tolerance, and adjust PT/orthotics; provide earlier return precautions for red flags.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and clear next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent asks about symptom onset and tempo and explicitly distinguishes slow progression typical of CMT from rapid/subacute progression that warrants reconsideration.
Agent screens for red flags requiring prompt reassessment: rapid progression, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, marked asymmetry, and new proximal weakness.
Agent elicits family history and discusses inherited nature of CMT and offers/encourages genetic counseling/testing options for patient and family members.
Agent explains that diagnosis is supported by exam plus nerve conduction studies/EMG and may be confirmed by genetic testing, avoiding overpromising certainty without testing when not yet done.
Agent states there is no curative therapy and frames management goals around function, safety, and symptom control.
Agent recommends PT/OT and orthoses (e.g., AFO) for gait improvement and fall reduction, and discusses periodic reassessment of orthotics/braces.
Agent provides foot care and skin-check counseling and appropriate footwear guidance to prevent ulcers and injuries.
Agent provides fall-prevention strategies and home modification advice appropriate to L1 difficulty (clear, practical, non-technical).
Agent gives exercise guidance emphasizing consistent low-impact activity and stretching and cautions against overexertion that worsens symptoms.
If neuropathic pain treatment is offered, agent explains gabapentin titration and monitoring for sedation/dizziness and advises on safety (e.g., driving/falls) during initiation.
Agent discusses when surgical referral may be considered (severe foot deformity/instability) without presenting it as routine or urgent in stable cases.
Before prescribing, agent performs an allergy check (critical) and documents/acknowledges the result.

Before prescribing, agent performs a contraindication check (critical) and avoids prescribing if contraindications are present; if the tool is unavailable, agent explicitly queries contraindications in history and documents reasoning.
Before prescribing, agent performs a drug interaction check (non-critical) or provides a rationale if not performed and mitigates risk (e.g., warns about CNS depressants).

Before ordering tests/medications, agent considers contraindications to ordering (non-critical) such as pregnancy status for certain tests if relevant, renal function considerations for medication dosing, and patient-specific limitations; if not applicable, agent states why.
Agent avoids recommending potentially neurotoxic medications when possible and counsels patient to inform clinicians/pharmacists about CMT when new meds are prescribed.
Agent uses shared decision-making, checks understanding, and provides clear follow-up and return precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Classic CMT presentation with minimal pain; focus on confirmation plan (NCS/EMG ± genetics) and supportive care (PT/OT, AFO, fall prevention, foot care) without prescribing.
must_collect: distal lower extremity weakness/foot drop or tripping
family history of similar symptoms/foot deformities
red flags absence (rapid progression, bowel/bladder changes, marked asymmetry, proximal weakness)
steps: ASK: symptoms, function, progression, red flags
ASK: family history, prior evaluations, comorbidities, medication exposures
ORDER_LAB: basic neuropathy screen if appropriate
DIAGNOSE: working diagnosis CMT (or suspected CMT pending studies)
EDUCATE: explain treatment (PT/OT, AFO, surgery criteria)
EDUCATE: lifestyle (foot care, falls, exercise, orthotics reassessment)
EDUCATE: address concerns (inheritance, expectations, med safety/neurotoxic avoidance)

minimal_paths.description: Classic CMT presentation with bothersome neuropathic pain; add safe gabapentin initiation after required safety checks.
must_collect: neuropathic pain features (burning/shooting, worse at night, stocking distribution)
pain severity and functional impact/sleep impact
current meds/substances that increase sedation (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines)
slowly progressive course and absence of red flags
steps: ASK: symptoms, progression, red flags, pain characterization
ASK: meds/substances, comorbidities (including renal), family history
ORDER_LAB: basic neuropathy screen if appropriate
EDUCATE: explain treatment incl gabapentin option and side effects
PRESCRIBE: gabapentin with titration/monitoring and fall/drive precautions
EDUCATE: lifestyle + foot care + fall prevention + exercise
EDUCATE: address concerns (inheritance, expectations, neurotoxic drug avoidance)

minimal_paths.description: Presentation includes red flags or atypical features; agent avoids premature CMT closure and escalates evaluation/urgent reassessment plan.
must_collect: systemic symptoms or acute onset features
steps: ASK: symptoms with explicit red-flag screening
ORDER_LAB: targeted workup for alternative causes as indicated
DIAGNOSE: broaden differential; do not label as straightforward CMT
EDUCATE: explain concern about atypical features and need for prompt reassessment/urgent evaluation
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: expedited (or direct to urgent/emergency care depending on severity)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders NCS/EMG first and defers basic neuropathy labs in a strongly hereditary, classic phenotype with clear family history, documenting rationale.

Agent provides conservative pain management counseling (sleep hygiene, PT strategies) and defers medication initiation to follow-up after testing or after trial of non-pharmacologic options, while still covering required education milestones.

If prescribing any medication, agent performs allergy + contraindication checks before prescribing; if the benchmark only exposes allergy/interaction tools, contraindications are assessed via ASK and explicitly documented before PRESCRIBE.
